questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the five SOLID principles?
questions.explanation: SOLID = SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP. The Law of Demeter (Principle of Least Knowledge) is a separate supporting principle. (OOPrinciples p.3, p.15)

questions.stem: One activity runs continuously, wrapping all seven phases of the change process from start to finish. Which activity is it?
questions.explanation: Verification is the continuous activity wrapping all seven phases. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: At the very centre of the Clean Architecture's concentric rings sits which layer?
questions.explanation: Entities (enterprise-wide business rules) are the innermost layer. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: The three sub-activities of actualization are implement, incorporate, and what?
questions.explanation: Actualization = implement + incorporate + propagate. (ActLab; Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: The formula TP/(TP+FP) gives which metric?
questions.explanation: TP/(TP+FP) is precision. (Actualization p.26)

questions.stem: A class has exactly 'one reason to change.' Which SOLID principle is satisfied?
questions.explanation: One reason to change is the definition of SRP. (OOPrinciples p.4)

questions.stem: Actualization is the implementation phase of which overall process?
questions.explanation: Actualization is the implementation phase of the software change process. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: On the outer edge of the Clean Architecture's concentric rings sits which layer?
questions.explanation: Frameworks & Drivers (web, DB, UI, HTTP client) is the outermost layer. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: Plugging newly written classes into existing code as components is called what?
questions.explanation: Plugging new classes into old code as components is incorporation. (Actualization p.8)

questions.stem: The formula TP/(TP+FN) gives which metric?
questions.explanation: TP/(TP+FN) is recall. (Actualization p.27)

questions.stem: Adding new features without modifying existing classes embodies which SOLID principle?
questions.explanation: Open for extension, closed for modification is OCP. (OOPrinciples p.6)

questions.stem: In which direction do source-code dependencies point under the Dependency Rule?
questions.explanation: Dependencies point only inward, toward the stable Entities. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: A change spreading to dependent components is known as the what?
questions.explanation: A change spreading through dependencies is the ripple effect. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: Which metric did the Ericsson programmers achieve at 100%?
questions.explanation: Ericsson achieved 100% precision (no false alarms). (Actualization p.26)

questions.stem: 'Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types' is which principle?
questions.explanation: Substitutability of subtypes for base types is LSP. (OOPrinciples p.8)

questions.stem: The database, per the Clean Architecture deck, is best described as a what?
questions.explanation: The database is a detail / plug-in, not the center. (Clean Architecture p.16-17)

questions.stem: Change propagation is described as the 'moment of truth' for which earlier phase?
questions.explanation: Propagation confirms or refutes Impact Analysis - the moment of truth. (Actualization p.23)

questions.stem: Which metric did the Ericsson programmers achieve at only 32%?
questions.explanation: Ericsson achieved only 32% recall (missed two-thirds of changed classes). (Actualization p.27)

questions.stem: 'Many client-specific interfaces beat one general-purpose interface' is which principle?
questions.explanation: Preferring many cohesive client-specific interfaces is ISP. (OOPrinciples p.10)

questions.stem: Use Cases in the Clean Architecture are also called what?
questions.explanation: Use Cases are also called interactors (application-specific business rules). (Clean Architecture p.7, p.9)

questions.stem: The deck's small-change example widens a ZIP field from how many digits to how many?
questions.explanation: The Address ZIP field is widened from zip[5] to zip[9]. (Actualization p.2-4)

questions.stem: Missed impacts that should have been flagged but were not - the dangerous ones - land in which confusion-matrix cell?
questions.explanation: False negatives (predicted-unchanged but actually-changed) are the dangerous misses. (Actualization p.25)

questions.stem: 'Depend upon abstractions, not concretions' is which principle?
questions.explanation: Depending on abstractions, not concretions, is DIP. (OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: Which item is named as a software ARCHITECTURE rather than a design pattern?
questions.explanation: Onion is an architecture; Singleton/Observer/MVVM are patterns. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: In the polymorphism example, what sound does the new Pig subclass make?
questions.explanation: The Pig subclass's makeSound() prints 'Oink'. (Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: What does a false positive represent in impact analysis?

options.text: A class predicted to change that did not change
A class predicted not to change that changed
A class correctly predicted to change

questions.explanation: A false positive is a class predicted to change that did not - a false alarm. Ericsson had FP = 0. (Actualization p.25)

questions.stem: 'Favor composition/delegation over inheritance as the reuse mechanism' is which principle?
questions.explanation: Favoring composition over inheritance is the Composite Reuse Principle. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: Which pattern is the Entity Gateway also known as?
questions.explanation: The Entity Gateway is the Repository pattern. (Clean Architecture p.7, p.18)

questions.stem: Which incorporation shape is the SIMPLEST, with new code as a self-contained leaf?
questions.explanation: The local shape (self-contained leaf) is the simplest incorporation case. (Actualization p.9)

questions.stem: Talking only to immediate collaborators (not transitively obtained objects) follows which principle?

options.text: Principle of Least Knowledge / Law of Demeter

questions.explanation: Calling only your immediate collaborators is the Principle of Least Knowledge (Law of Demeter). (OOPrinciples p.15)

questions.stem: Hexagonal, Onion, and Clean are examples of what?
questions.explanation: Hexagonal, Onion, and Clean are architectures. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: 'Put the behaviour where the data lives' summarises which GRASP pattern?
questions.explanation: Assigning behaviour to the class holding the data is Information Expert. (OOPrinciples p.19)

questions.stem: MVC, MVVM, and MVP are examples of what?
questions.explanation: MVC, MVVM, and MVP are design patterns. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: 'Who creates object A?' is answered by which GRASP pattern?
questions.explanation: Assigning responsibility for creating instances is the Creator pattern. (OOPrinciples p.20)

questions.stem: The Clean Architecture is attributed to which author?
questions.explanation: Robert C. Martin is credited with the Clean Architecture. (Clean Architecture p.5)

questions.stem: 'Who handles a system event?' is answered by which GRASP pattern?
questions.explanation: Handling system events is assigned by the Controller pattern. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: Which layer holds 'enterprise-wide business rules that are least likely to change'?
questions.explanation: Entities hold enterprise-wide rules that are least likely to change. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: 'Behaviour varies by type' is best handled by which GRASP pattern?
questions.explanation: Type-varying behaviour is assigned via GRASP Polymorphism. (OOPrinciples p.24)

questions.stem: 'Use an intermediate object so two components are not directly coupled' is which GRASP pattern?
questions.explanation: Mediating via an intermediate object is Indirection. (OOPrinciples p.25)

questions.stem: 'Invent a made-up class (not a domain concept) to hold cohesive logic' is which GRASP pattern?
questions.explanation: Inventing a non-domain class for cohesion is Pure Fabrication. (OOPrinciples p.26)

questions.stem: 'Wrap a predicted point of change behind a stable interface' is which GRASP pattern?
questions.explanation: Wrapping predicted variation behind a stable interface is Protected Variations. (OOPrinciples p.27)

questions.stem: 'Keep a class's reliance on other classes small' is which GRASP goal?
questions.explanation: Minimising reliance on other classes is the Low Coupling goal. (OOPrinciples p.21)

questions.stem: 'Keep a class's responsibilities focused and related' is which GRASP goal?
questions.explanation: Keeping responsibilities focused is the High Cohesion goal. (OOPrinciples p.22)

questions.stem: GRASP stands for General Responsibility Assignment Software ___?
questions.explanation: GRASP = General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns. (OOPrinciples p.16)

questions.stem: How many principles make up SOLID?
questions.explanation: SOLID is five principles: SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP. (OOPrinciples p.3)

questions.stem: The Principle of Least Knowledge is also known as the Law of what?
questions.explanation: The Principle of Least Knowledge is also the Law of Demeter. (OOPrinciples p.15)

questions.stem: A developer widens a single Address field from a 5-digit to a 9-digit ZIP, touching only that one class and adding no new structure. Which actualization technique is in play?

options.text: Writing a new class separately and incorporating it as a supplier
Polymorphic extension of a composite responsibility via a new subclass
A small change edited directly into the existing old code
Replacement of an obsolete class and rerouting all references

questions.explanation: A localized one-field edit with no new structure is the deck's canonical SMALL change, done directly in old code. The other options describe larger-change shapes. (Actualization p.2-4)

questions.stem: A designer wants a 'change the look of the login screen' request to ripple as little as possible. Which architectural property guarantees the ripple stays in the outer layer?
questions.explanation: UI-independence means the UI can change without changing other components, so a UI-only request ripples only through the outer layer. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: A team must support a new loan type. With OCP applied, the LoanApprovalHandler depends on a Validator interface. What do they do to add the new type?

options.text: Add a new class implementing Validator, leaving LoanApprovalHandler unchanged
Edit LoanApprovalHandler to add an else-if branch
Subclass LoanApprovalHandler for each loan type

questions.explanation: OCP keeps the handler closed to modification but open to new validators: add a new Validator implementer, no edit to the handler. (OOPrinciples p.6-7)

questions.stem: A maintainer adds a new TriangleFigure to JHotDraw by writing a class that extends the Figure abstraction; DrawingView and DrawingEditor are untouched. Which principle does this exemplify?
questions.explanation: Adding a Figure subclass without editing clients is the textbook OCP / GRASP-Polymorphism case in JHotDraw. (Actualization p.6-7; OOPrinciples p.6-7)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson result, how did precision and recall compare?

options.text: Precision was low (32%) while recall was perfect (100%)
Precision was perfect (100%) while recall was low (32%)

questions.explanation: Ericsson achieved 100% precision (no false alarms) but only 32% recall (missed two-thirds of changed classes). (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: What is the practical meaning difference between high precision and high recall for an impact prediction?

options.text: High precision means no missed classes; high recall means no false alarms
High recall means the prediction was small
High precision means no false alarms; high recall means no missed classes

questions.explanation: Precision = no false alarms (everything flagged really changed); recall = no misses (everything that changed was flagged). (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: Just before Actualization, one phase readies a clean insertion point for the new code. Which phase plays that setup role?
questions.explanation: Prefactoring precedes Actualization and prepares a clean place to plug the new code in; Postfactoring follows. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: A change request adds an entirely new authentication responsibility that did not exist before. A programmer wants to write and test it cleanly before wiring it in. Which strategy matches the deck?

options.text: Edit the launch code in place because it is a one-line guard
Write the new class separately, then incorporate it into the existing code
Delete the old launch class and let propagation rebuild it
Add an if/else branch to every existing client class

questions.explanation: A brand-new responsibility is a LARGER change: implement the new class separately, then plug it in (incorporation). Building it in isolation lets you get it right and testable first. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the four characteristics of a successful (Clean) architecture?

options.text: Dependent on a specific framework for speed

questions.explanation: The four characteristics are testable and independent of UI, DB, and frameworks/external entities - the opposite of being tied to a framework. (Clean Architecture p.3-4)

questions.stem: A class Payments currently writes 'new CreditCardPaymentMethod()' inside itself. A reviewer asks for it to accept any payment method via the constructor. Which principle is the reviewer enforcing?
questions.explanation: Depending on the PaymentMethod abstraction injected via the constructor, instead of newing a concrete class, is DIP via constructor injection. (OOPrinciples p.12-13)

questions.stem: Dropping a brand-new Figure type into JHotDraw without disturbing DrawingView or DrawingEditor exemplifies which actualization shape?

options.text: Polymorphic extension of a composite responsibility

questions.explanation: Adding a leaf Figure subclass without editing clients is the 'add a Pig, don't edit Farm' polymorphic-extension shape. (Actualization p.6-7 applied)

questions.stem: Given the Ericsson confusion matrix with TP=30, FP=0, TN=42, FN=64, compute the precision.
questions.explanation: Precision = TP/(TP+FP) = 30/(30+0) = 100%. (Actualization p.26)

questions.stem: During Actualization, the predicted impact set from Impact Analysis corresponds to what?

options.text: The classes that can never change
The set of classes you now expect to touch during incorporation and propagation

questions.explanation: The predicted impact set is exactly the classes expected to be touched during incorporation and propagation. (Actualization p.1, p.23)

questions.stem: A team adds a Pig type to a Farm of FarmAnimals by writing a new Pig subclass that overrides makeSound(); the Farm class is left untouched. What actualization shape is this?

options.text: Incorporating a new supplier that all clients must point to
A small direct edit inside the Farm class
Replacement of the FarmAnimal base class
Polymorphic extension of a composite responsibility

questions.explanation: Adding a leaf subclass that implements an existing operation differently, without editing clients, is polymorphic extension of Farm's composite responsibility. (Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT named as an example of a software ARCHITECTURE in the deck?
questions.explanation: Observer is a design pattern, not an architecture. The deck lists Hexagonal, Onion, and Clean as architectures. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: In a UML interaction diagram, a Sale aggregates SaleLineItems. A designer must decide who creates SaleLineItem objects. Which GRASP pattern points to Sale?
questions.explanation: Creator: assign creation of A to a class that aggregates/contains A. Since Sale aggregates SaleLineItems, Sale creates them. (OOPrinciples p.20)

questions.stem: From the Ericsson matrix (TP=30, FP=0, TN=42, FN=64), work out the recall figure.
questions.explanation: Recall = TP/(TP+FN) = 30/(30+64) = 0.32 = 32%. (Actualization p.27)

questions.stem: A programmer removes an obsolete report-generation feature and assumes deletion is 'free' since no new code is added. What does the deck say will happen?

options.text: Nothing - deletion never propagates because it removes code
Only the deleted class's tests need updating
Impact analysis is skipped for deletions
All references to the deleted functionality must be removed, and secondary changes propagate

questions.explanation: Deletion also propagates: all references to the deleted functionality must be deleted, and secondary changes ripple to other classes. (Actualization p.22)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT listed as a design PATTERN in the Clean Architecture deck?
questions.explanation: Onion is an architecture, not a design pattern. The deck's pattern examples are Singleton, Observer, MVC, MVVM, MVP. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: A class needs to total a sale, and the Sale object is the one that holds all the line-item data. Which GRASP pattern says Sale should own getTotal()?
questions.explanation: Information Expert assigns a responsibility to the class that has the information to fulfill it; Sale holds the line items, so Sale totals. (OOPrinciples p.19)

questions.stem: From the Ericsson matrix (TP=30, FP=0, TN=42, FN=64), how many classes ACTUALLY changed in total?
questions.explanation: Actually changed = TP + FN = 30 + 64 = 94. (Actualization p.24-25)

questions.stem: A maintainer finishes incorporating a feature and the system is finally internally consistent again - no inconsistent classes remain. What has just occurred?

options.text: The ripple effect has been disabled

questions.explanation: Change propagation ends precisely when no inconsistent classes remain and the system is consistent again. (Actualization p.21)

questions.stem: Which item does NOT belong to the outermost Frameworks & Drivers layer?
questions.explanation: Enterprise business rules live in the innermost Entities layer, not the outer Frameworks & Drivers layer (web, DB, UI, HTTP client). (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the nine GRASP patterns?
questions.explanation: Dependency Inversion is a SOLID principle, not a GRASP pattern. The nine GRASP are Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Controller, Polymorphism, Indirection, Pure Fabrication, Protected Variations. (OOPrinciples p.18)

questions.stem: From the Ericsson matrix (TP=30, FP=0, TN=42, FN=64), how many classes were PREDICTED to change in total?
questions.explanation: Predicted changed = TP + FP = 30 + 0 = 30. (Actualization p.24-25)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the three sub-activities of the Actualization phase?

options.text: Incorporating the new code into old code

questions.explanation: Actualization = implement + incorporate + propagate. Concept Location is an earlier phase, not part of actualization. (ActLab; Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: How does an architecture differ from a design pattern, per the deck?

options.text: Architecture organises the whole-system structure; a pattern is a reusable solution to a local design problem
Patterns organise the whole system; architecture solves local problems
Patterns are always concentric; architectures are never layered

questions.explanation: Architecture is the high-level whole-system structure (Hexagonal/Onion/Clean); a design pattern is a reusable form of a solution to a local problem (Singleton/Observer/MVC). (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a supporting (non-SOLID) principle named in the deck?
questions.explanation: SRP is part of SOLID. The supporting principles are the Composite Reuse Principle and the Principle of Least Knowledge / Law of Demeter. (OOPrinciples p.14-15)

questions.stem: From the Ericsson matrix (TP=30, FP=0, TN=42, FN=64), what is the TOTAL number of classes?
questions.explanation: Total = 42 + 0 + 64 + 30 = 136. (Actualization p.24)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the three structural shapes of incorporation shown in the deck?
questions.explanation: The three shapes are local, composite, and incorporating a new supplier. 'Inverting the dependency graph' is not one of them. (Actualization p.9-11)

questions.stem: How do the Entities layer and the Use Cases layer differ in the rules they hold?

options.text: Entities hold enterprise-wide (application-independent) rules; Use Cases hold application-specific rules
Entities hold application-specific rules; Use Cases hold enterprise-wide rules

questions.explanation: Entities = enterprise-wide, application-independent rules (least likely to change); Use Cases/interactors = application-specific rules. (Clean Architecture p.7, p.9)

questions.stem: Under the Law of Demeter, which object's methods may an operation O on class C NOT freely call?

options.text: Methods on an object returned by calling a method on another object

questions.explanation: Demeter forbids calling methods on objects obtained transitively (via a method call on another object); allowed targets are self, parameters, created objects, and contained fields. (OOPrinciples p.15)

questions.stem: A new prediction has TP=25, FP=5, FN=0. Which best describes it relative to Ericsson?

options.text: Better recall than Ericsson (100% vs 32%) but slightly worse precision (~83% vs 100%)

questions.explanation: Recall = 25/25 = 100% (beats Ericsson's 32%); precision = 25/30 = ~83% (below Ericsson's 100%). (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: What is the consequence of incorporating a new class at the incorporation point?

options.text: The system stays consistent with no further work
Dependents become inconsistent and must be visited and updated as the modified frontier advances

questions.explanation: Incorporation makes dependents inconsistent; the modified frontier advances class by class until consistency returns. (Actualization p.16-20)

questions.stem: What is the correct order of the four Clean Architecture layers from INNER (most stable) to OUTER (most volatile)?

options.text: Entities -> Use Cases -> Interface Adapters -> Frameworks & Drivers
Frameworks & Drivers -> Interface Adapters -> Use Cases -> Entities
Use Cases -> Entities -> Frameworks & Drivers -> Interface Adapters
Interface Adapters -> Frameworks & Drivers -> Entities -> Use Cases

questions.explanation: Inner to outer: Entities, Use Cases, Interface Adapters, Frameworks & Drivers. (Clean Architecture p.5-7)

questions.stem: A class both formats invoices AND computes tax, so it has two unrelated reasons to change. Which principle is violated?
questions.explanation: Two unrelated reasons to change in one class violates SRP - a class should have only one reason to change. (OOPrinciples p.4)

questions.stem: For a change that adds a brand-new responsibility plus its own helper classes, which actualization technique is most appropriate?

options.text: Edit the change directly into a single old class
Delete an existing class to make room
Write the new classes separately, then incorporate them (larger-change technique)
Skip incorporation and rely on propagation alone

questions.explanation: A larger change with new structure is implemented separately and then incorporated. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: Which term-to-category pairing is CORRECT?
questions.explanation: Onion is an architecture; MVC and Singleton are patterns, and Clean is an architecture (not a pattern). (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: A single fat IUser interface forces every implementer to provide changePassword(), checkUserRole() AND assignRole() even when unneeded. Which principle is violated?
questions.explanation: Forcing implementers to carry methods they don't need is an ISP violation; split into client-specific cohesive interfaces. (OOPrinciples p.10-11)

questions.stem: A teammate claims 'small changes never propagate.' Why is that wrong?

options.text: Small changes always propagate to every class
Even a direct edit can ripple, and deletion definitely propagates - all references must be removed
Small changes skip the propagation step entirely
Only larger changes touch any other class

questions.explanation: Small changes can still ripple, and deletion definitely propagates (all references to deleted functionality must be removed). (Actualization p.22)

questions.stem: Which layer converts data between the inner format and the format needed by the DB and web?
questions.explanation: Interface Adapters convert data between inner use-case/entity format and DB/web format (Presenters, ViewModels, Gateways). (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: A Payments class instantiates a concrete CreditCardPaymentMethod with 'new' inside itself, welding it to credit-card logic. Which principle is violated?
questions.explanation: Newing a concrete class welds high-level code to a low-level detail - a DIP violation; depend on the abstraction instead. (OOPrinciples p.12-13)

questions.stem: In actualization, what is the correct order of the three sub-activities?
questions.explanation: Actualization runs implement (write new code), then incorporate (plug it in), then propagate (fix secondary changes). (ActLab; Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: The Delivery Mechanism in the Clean Architecture data flow can be which of the following?

options.text: Web, a console app, or a mobile app UI

questions.explanation: The Delivery Mechanism can be Web, a console application, a mobile app UI, etc. (Clean Architecture p.9)

questions.stem: A class leans on a dozen collaborators, so unrelated edits ripple into it and reuse in isolation is painful. Which GRASP goal has been broken?
questions.explanation: Reliance on many classes (fragile, hard to reuse) is the symptom of high coupling - the Low Coupling goal is violated. (OOPrinciples p.21)

questions.stem: In the PoS cashier-login walkthrough, which step comes right after implementing the new Cashiers class in isolation?

options.text: Incorporating it (plugging it into the model and wiring the launch path)

questions.explanation: After implementing the new class separately, you incorporate it - add it to the model and wire the launch path to require login. (Actualization p.13-14)

questions.stem: Where, in the Clean Architecture data flow, is presentable formatting (e.g. a date string) produced?
questions.explanation: The Presenter turns the non-presentable Response Model into a presentable View Model (e.g. date strings). (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: A class juggles many unrelated jobs and far too much work, leaving it hard to grasp and perpetually disturbed by change. Which GRASP goal has been broken?
questions.explanation: Doing many unrelated things (delicate, hard to maintain) is low cohesion - the High Cohesion goal is violated. (OOPrinciples p.22)

questions.stem: In the deck's polymorphism example, which subclasses exist on the Farm BEFORE Pig is added?

options.text: Cow, Sheep, and (then) Pig is the new one added to Cow/Sheep/Pig

questions.explanation: The Farm holds Cow and Sheep; adding Pig extends Farm's composite responsibility. (Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: What does the Interface Adapters layer DO, in one phrase?

options.text: Convert data between the inner (entity/use-case) format and the DB/web format

questions.explanation: Interface Adapters convert data between the convenient inner format and the format needed by DBs and the web. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: Given the responsibility 'put the behaviour where the necessary data already lives', which GRASP pattern is it?
questions.explanation: Assigning a responsibility to the class that has the information to fulfill it is Information Expert. (OOPrinciples p.19)

questions.stem: In the Point-of-Sale incorporation example, which new class is added to the model (Store, Inventory, Item, Price) to satisfy the login change request?
questions.explanation: A Cashiers class (holding credentials and login) is added to the model and incorporated. (Actualization p.13)

questions.stem: Which characteristic of a clean architecture most directly enables writing business-rule tests that don't touch the UI or DB?
questions.explanation: Testability means business rules have tests independent of UI, DB and external elements. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: What does applying SRP do for change propagation during actualization?

options.text: It widens propagation by spreading one responsibility across classes
It has no effect on propagation
It limits propagation, since each class has one responsibility so a change touches few classes

questions.explanation: With one responsibility per class, a change request touches few classes, shortening ripple and improving impact-analysis recall. (OOPrinciples p.4-5)

questions.stem: Which statement best describes what triggers the start of change propagation?

options.text: Modifying a class at the incorporation point, which makes its dependents inconsistent
Writing the new class in isolation

questions.explanation: Propagation starts when a class is modified at the incorporation point and its dependents become inconsistent. (Actualization p.16)

questions.stem: For business rules, which reading best captures 'independent of any external agency'?

options.text: Business rules drive the UI directly
Business rules should know nothing about the outside world
Business rules live in the database
Business rules depend on the web framework

questions.explanation: Independence from external agencies means business rules know nothing about the outside world. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: Who should be responsible for CREATING a SaleLineItem object, given a Sale that aggregates them? Choose the best GRASP assignment.

options.text: The Controller, since it handles events
The Sale, because it aggregates SaleLineItems (Creator)
The View, since it displays them

questions.explanation: Creator: the class that aggregates/contains A (Sale) is the best place to create A (SaleLineItem). (OOPrinciples p.20)

questions.stem: Per the deck, why is removing obsolete code not 'free'?

options.text: Because deletion never affects other classes
Because deletion requires a new class
Because all references to the deleted functionality must be removed and secondary changes propagate

questions.explanation: Deletion propagates: every reference must be removed and secondary changes ripple. (Actualization p.22)

questions.stem: Which best states why the database being a plug-in keeps a 'switch DB engine' change cheap?

options.text: Because the change is confined to the Entity Gateway Implementation and cannot reach the entities or use cases
Because the entities are rewritten each time
Because the View handles the database
Because the Request Model stores SQL

questions.explanation: A plug-in DB behind the gateway confines a swap to the gateway implementation, never reaching the inner layers. (Clean Architecture p.16-18)

questions.stem: A subsystem must handle a 'process sale' system event coming from the UI. Which GRASP pattern best assigns this responsibility?
questions.explanation: Controller assigns system-event handling to a facade or use-case/session controller - not to the UI. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: When incorporated new classes 'assume the responsibilities demanded by the change request,' what does this describe?
questions.explanation: New classes plugged in as components assume the change request's responsibilities - that is incorporation. (Actualization p.8)

questions.stem: A 'switch the GUI toolkit' request should, in a clean design, leave which layer completely untouched?
questions.explanation: The Entities (and Use Cases) stay untouched; only the outer layers change for a GUI swap. (Clean Architecture p.4, p.16-18)

questions.stem: A class needs the data to compute an order's total and that data already lives in the Order. Which GRASP pattern best places getTotal()?

options.text: Information Expert - the Order holds the data, so it computes the total

questions.explanation: Information Expert places behaviour on the class holding the needed information; the Order holds line items, so it totals them. (OOPrinciples p.19)

questions.stem: A maintainer chooses between editing in place and writing a new class. What single factor does the deck say drives this choice?
questions.explanation: The deck's central idea: the size of the change dictates the technique. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: Which best describes the Boundary in the Clean Architecture data flow?

options.text: An interface/protocol through which data enters and leaves the interactor

questions.explanation: A Boundary is an interface/protocol; data enters/leaves the interactor through boundaries. (Clean Architecture p.9)

questions.stem: A teammate cites CRP to argue 'we should never use inheritance.' Why is that an overstatement?

options.text: CRP bans inheritance in all cases
CRP says inheritance is always preferred
CRP favors composition over inheritance-as-primary-reuse, but inheritance is still correct for genuine is-a polymorphism (Farm/Pig, OCP)

questions.explanation: CRP warns against inheritance-as-primary-reuse, not against all inheritance; the Farm/Pig and OCP examples legitimately use inheritance. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: Which set lists the four characteristics of clean architecture correctly?

options.text: Testable; independent of UI; independent of DB; independent of frameworks/external entities
Testable; coupled to UI; coupled to DB; coupled to frameworks

questions.explanation: The four characteristics: testable and independent of UI, DB, and frameworks/external entities. (Clean Architecture p.3-4)

questions.stem: Someone says there are '5 SOLID principles and 5 GRASP patterns.' What is the correct count?
questions.explanation: SOLID has exactly five principles; GRASP has exactly nine patterns. Don't merge or miscount the lists. (OOPrinciples p.3, p.18)

questions.stem: Which scenario is a 'replacement of a class' rather than a fresh incorporation?

options.text: Adding a brand-new leaf logging class nothing else calls
Widening a ZIP field in place
A new class takes over an old class's role and all references are rerouted to it
Adding a Pig subclass to Farm

questions.explanation: Replacement has a new class assume an old class's role with references rerouted, then the obsolete class is deleted. (Actualization p.15)

questions.stem: A student attributes the SOLID acronym to Robert C. Martin. What is the correct attribution?

options.text: Martin coined both the principles and the acronym
The acronym was coined by the Gang of Four
Martin introduced the principles (2000 paper); Michael Feathers coined the SOLID acronym

questions.explanation: Martin introduced the principles in his 2000 paper; the SOLID acronym itself was coined later by Michael Feathers. (OOPrinciples p.2)

questions.stem: A student attributes GRASP to Robert C. Martin. Who actually introduced GRASP?

options.text: Craig Larman, in Applying UML and Patterns

questions.explanation: GRASP was introduced by Craig Larman in Applying UML and Patterns; Martin authored SOLID and Clean Architecture. (OOPrinciples p.16-17)

questions.stem: In the SRP deck example, how is the violation fixed when UserService both changes passwords and verifies access?

options.text: Merge both responsibilities into one larger method
Split access checking into a separate SecurityService
Make UserService a subclass of SecurityService

questions.explanation: Access checking is extracted into its own SecurityService so each class has one reason to change. (OOPrinciples p.5)

questions.stem: In the ISP deck example, the fat IUser interface (changePassword, checkUserRole, assignRole) is fixed by splitting into which interfaces?
questions.explanation: It is split into cohesive client-specific interfaces: IUser (changePassword), IRole (assignRole), IUserRole (checkUserRole). (OOPrinciples p.11)

questions.stem: Reading this code: 'class Payments { PaymentMethod pm; Payments(PaymentMethod p){ pm=p; } void makePayments(){ pm.transact(); } }' - which principle does this CORRECTLY apply?

options.text: It applies DIP via constructor injection

questions.explanation: Depending on the PaymentMethod abstraction injected through the constructor is DIP done correctly. (OOPrinciples p.13)

questions.stem: A reviewer replaces a deep inheritance hierarchy with a delegate that can be swapped at runtime. Which principle motivates this?
questions.explanation: Favoring runtime-swappable composition/delegation over inheritance is the Composite Reuse Principle. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: Given responsibilities 'evaluate whether this design has a class relying on too many others,' which GRASP goal are you applying?
questions.explanation: Judging reliance on other classes is the Low Coupling evaluative goal. (OOPrinciples p.21)

questions.stem: The phrase 'the interactor controls the dance of the entities' refers to what?

options.text: The Use Case orchestrating the application-independent entities to fulfil a use case

questions.explanation: The interactor (use case) orchestrates - 'controls the dance of' - the entities to carry out the use case. (Clean Architecture p.11)

questions.stem: A developer must add a brand-new figure type that varies its draw() behaviour by subtype. Combining the deck's lessons, which is the cleanest plan?

options.text: Add a type field and a switch in the base class
Put draw() logic in the database
Duplicate the base class for each type
Add a subclass implementing draw() polymorphically (OCP + GRASP Polymorphism), leaving clients untouched

questions.explanation: Add a polymorphic subclass (OCP + GRASP Polymorphism) so clients stay untouched - the least-rippling actualization. (OOPrinciples p.6, p.24; Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: A reviewer wants the design to depend on a PaymentMethod interface so any concrete method can be plugged in later. This is OCP achieved through which principle?
questions.explanation: Depending on the abstraction to stay open to new methods is DIP, which the deck says is how you adhere to OCP. (OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: Read off the five SOLID letters in order. Which expansion is correct end to end?
questions.explanation: SOLID = Single-responsibility, Open/closed, Liskov substitution, Interface segregation, Dependency inversion. (OOPrinciples p.3)

questions.stem: Given the responsibility 'choose which class should INSTANTIATE a part that the whole closely uses,' which GRASP pattern applies?
questions.explanation: Creator assigns instantiation to a class that closely uses (or aggregates/contains/records/has init data for) the part. (OOPrinciples p.20)

questions.stem: A maintainer reduces a god-class that 'does too much work and many unrelated things.' Which GRASP goal is being improved?
questions.explanation: Reducing a class that does too much/unrelated work improves High Cohesion. (OOPrinciples p.22)

questions.stem: When the assignment of responsibilities happens during interaction-diagram creation and programming, which body of patterns is the deck describing?
questions.explanation: GRASP responsibility assignment often occurs during interaction-diagram creation and certainly during programming. (OOPrinciples p.16)

questions.stem: A class is hard to reuse because using it drags in many other classes it depends on. Which GRASP goal does this symptom indicate is poor?
questions.explanation: Needing many other classes to reuse one indicates high coupling - poor Low Coupling. (OOPrinciples p.21)

questions.stem: A developer is told to 'reduce coupling to the abstract level by relating a concrete class to an interface instead of to another concrete class.' Which principle is this a tenet of?
questions.explanation: Reducing coupling to the abstract level (concrete-to-interface) is a tenet of OCP. (OOPrinciples p.6)

questions.stem: After plugging a new Cashiers class into the Point-of-Sale launch path, the old 'anyone can launch' code is now inconsistent. What must the programmer do next?

options.text: Stop, since incorporation completes the actualization phase
Re-run Concept Location to find the starting class again
Immediately delete the Store and Inventory classes
Walk the dependency graph updating every inconsistent dependent until consistency is restored

questions.explanation: Incorporation makes dependents inconsistent; change propagation then visits and updates each one until no inconsistent class remains. (Actualization p.16-21)

questions.stem: A product owner requests switching the persistence store from a relational DB to NoSQL. In a Clean Architecture, where is this change actualized?

options.text: Entirely in the Entity Gateway Implementation in the detail layer
In the Entities layer, since they own the data
In the Use Cases / interactors, which control the data
Spread across all four layers equally

questions.explanation: Because the DB is a plug-in behind a gateway interface, the swap is confined to the Entity Gateway Implementation; propagation cannot reach entities or use cases. (Clean Architecture p.16-18)

questions.stem: A teammate proposes assigning the responsibility to save objects to a relational database to a domain Entity. A senior dev objects and creates a made-up PersistentStorage class instead. Which GRASP pattern did the senior dev apply?
questions.explanation: Persistence is assigned to an artificial, highly-cohesive class that is not a domain concept - Pure Fabrication (e.g. PersistentStorage) - to keep entities clean. (OOPrinciples p.26)

questions.stem: A request asks to swap JHotDraw's Swing GUI for a different toolkit. If the architecture is clean, which layers should the ripple be confined to?

options.text: The Interface Adapters and Frameworks & Drivers (outer) layers

questions.explanation: The drawing model plays Entities/Use-Cases; the GUI plays the outer layers, so the Dependency Rule confines a toolkit swap to the outer layers. (Clean Architecture p.16-18)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson confusion matrix, which value is NOT zero?

options.text: Both FP and FN are zero

questions.explanation: FP = 0 (perfect precision) but FN = 64 (the dangerous misses). (Actualization p.24-25)

questions.stem: A maintainer plugs in a new logging class as a self-contained leaf: old code calls into it, but nothing new calls back out. Which incorporation shape is this?
questions.explanation: A self-contained leaf that old code calls into, with no further outward dependencies, is the LOCAL incorporation shape - the simplest case. (Actualization p.9)

questions.stem: Under the Dependency Rule, which of these is NOT allowed?

options.text: An outer Frameworks layer depending on an inner Use-Case
An Entity importing a class from the Frameworks & Drivers layer
A Use-Case depending on an Entity
An Interface Adapter depending on a Use-Case

questions.explanation: Dependencies point only inward; an inner Entity depending on an outer Frameworks class points outward and violates the rule. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: A use case must work whether figures are stored in SQL or NoSQL - a point of predicted variation. A developer wraps the store behind a stable interface. Which GRASP pattern is this?
questions.explanation: Identifying a predicted point of variation and wrapping it behind a stable interface is Protected Variations. (OOPrinciples p.27)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, the RectangleFigure/EllipseFigure/LineFigure hierarchy under a Figure abstraction best demonstrates which pair?

options.text: Law of Demeter and High Cohesion

questions.explanation: Adding a Figure subtype without editing clients exemplifies OCP and GRASP Polymorphism. (OOPrinciples p.6-7, p.24)

questions.stem: Set precision against recall on the basis of their denominators. Which statement gets that contrast right?

options.text: Precision divides by predicted-changed (TP+FP); recall divides by actually-changed (TP+FN)
Both divide by the number of predicted-changed classes
Precision divides by actually-changed; recall divides by predicted-changed
Both divide by the total number of classes

questions.explanation: Precision = TP/(TP+FP) uses predicted-changed as denominator; recall = TP/(TP+FN) uses actually-changed. (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: A new currency-conversion class is added, but it itself depends on a rate-table class and a rounding class, so incorporating it brings a small subtree. Which shape is this?
questions.explanation: When the new code itself depends on further components, incorporating it pulls in a subtree - the COMPOSITE shape, not a single leaf. (Actualization p.10)

questions.stem: Which statement is NOT consistent with the deck's view of the database?

options.text: The database should be a plug-in to the business rules
Business rules should be written in stored procedures
The DB should not be the center of the architecture

questions.explanation: The deck explicitly says business rules should NOT be written in stored procedures; the DB is a detail/plug-in. (Clean Architecture p.17)

questions.stem: A GUI window currently calls business logic directly to process a 'submit order' event. A reviewer insists the window delegate this to another class. Which GRASP pattern is the reviewer applying?
questions.explanation: Controller assigns system-event handling to a non-UI facade or use-case controller; window/view classes must delegate, not handle. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: Mapping JHotDraw onto Clean Architecture, the drawing model (Drawing, Figure, Handle) plays which role?
questions.explanation: The drawing model holds the application rules, playing the Entities/Use-Cases role; the Swing GUI plays the outer layers. (Clean Architecture p.5-8 applied)

questions.stem: Given a prediction that flagged every changed class but also flagged many unchanged ones, which metric is degraded?
questions.explanation: Flagging unchanged classes creates false positives, which lowers precision = TP/(TP+FP). Recall stays high since all changed classes were caught. (Actualization p.26)

questions.stem: How does a SMALL change differ from a LARGER change in actualization technique?

options.text: Small changes are written as new classes; larger changes are edited in place
Both are always edited directly in old code
Small changes are edited directly into old code; larger changes are written separately then incorporated
Both are always written as new classes

questions.explanation: Small changes are done directly in old code (ZIP example); larger changes write new classes separately and incorporate them. (Actualization p.2-5)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT something the View is supposed to do, per the deck?

options.text: Grab data from the View Model
Be 'so stupid you don't have to test it'
Format a date object into a display string

questions.explanation: Formatting (e.g. date strings) is the Presenter's job; the View only grabs view-model data and displays it. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: Per the GRASP Controller pattern, which class is explicitly NOT allowed to handle a system event?

options.text: A facade controller representing the overall system
A class representing a use-case scenario

questions.explanation: Controller excludes window, applet, widget, view, and document classes - they must delegate system events to a controller. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: To show DIP / Creator in JHotDraw, which figure-construction structure does the guide recommend?

options.text: Hard-coded 'new RectangleFigure()' calls scattered across views
Figures that save themselves to the database
A single god-class holding all figures

questions.explanation: The guide suggests injected/factory-created figures as the DIP/Creator example. (OOPrinciples p.12-13, p.20 applied)

questions.stem: What is the management consequence of low recall in impact analysis (Ericsson's 32%)?

options.text: Schedules become more accurate because fewer classes are flagged
Effort is under-estimated and schedules blow out, because two-thirds of the changed classes were missed

questions.explanation: Low recall means missed classes (false negatives), so effort is under-estimated, making planning difficult and blowing schedules. (Actualization p.27-28)

questions.stem: What is the consequence when a change propagation does NOT converge (inconsistent classes keep appearing)?

options.text: It confirms the design is excellent
It ends the actualization phase successfully

questions.explanation: Termination is the goal; a propagation that never converges signals a design problem (e.g. excessive coupling). (Actualization p.21)

questions.stem: How does the Request Model differ from the Response Model in the data flow?

options.text: Request Model carries primitive input into the interactor; Response Model carries the interactor's results out
Request Model carries results out; Response Model carries input in
Both are produced by the Presenter

questions.explanation: Request Model packs primitive input INTO the interactor; Response Model carries the interactor's (non-presentable) results OUT. (Clean Architecture p.10, p.12, p.15)

questions.stem: Place SRP beside ISP: how does their SCOPE differ?

options.text: SRP concerns interfaces; ISP concerns classes
SRP concerns a class having one reason to change; ISP concerns interfaces being client-specific and cohesive
SRP concerns inheritance; ISP concerns composition

questions.explanation: SRP is about a class having a single reason to change; ISP is about splitting fat interfaces into client-specific cohesive ones. Different scopes. (OOPrinciples p.4, p.10)

questions.stem: After actualizing 'add a new tool' in JHotDraw, which dependency chain do you walk to propagate secondary changes?
questions.explanation: You walk the Tool -> DrawingEditor -> DrawingView dependency chain, propagating until consistency is restored. (Actualization p.16-21 applied)

questions.stem: A student writes 'precision = 30/(30+64) = 32%.' Which error has crept in?

options.text: The arithmetic is wrong; it should be 50%
Precision and recall are the same thing here
They computed recall, not precision; precision divides by predicted-changed (TP+FP), not by actually-changed (TP+FN)

questions.explanation: 30/(30+64) uses the FN denominator, which is recall (32%). Precision = TP/(TP+FP) = 30/30 = 100%. (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: During the propagation walk, what happens to the 'modified frontier' as work proceeds?

options.text: It advances class by class as each inconsistent dependent is visited and updated
It stays fixed at the incorporation point
It shrinks only when a class is deleted

questions.explanation: The modified frontier advances class by class through the dependency graph until no inconsistent class remains. (Actualization p.17-20)

questions.stem: Given a violation where the Entities layer imports a Swing UI class, which architectural rule is broken?

options.text: The Dependency Rule (dependencies must point inward)

questions.explanation: An inner Entity importing an outer UI class points the dependency outward, violating the Dependency Rule. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: What is the essential relationship the deck states between DIP and OCP?

options.text: OCP tells us how to adhere to DIP
DIP tells us how we can adhere to OCP

questions.explanation: The deck states explicitly: 'DIP tells us how we can adhere to OCP' - depending on abstractions is the mechanism for being open to extension. (OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: Why does the Actualization lab make the JHotDraw/CASE-study link mandatory for SOLID and Clean Architecture?

options.text: To test memorisation of slide page numbers
Because JHotDraw replaces the change process
Because the lab forbids using JHotDraw
Because the portfolio deliverable requires concrete CASE-study examples of each SOLID principle and of Clean Architecture layering

questions.explanation: The lab requires SOLID examples and a Clean Architecture explanation in the context of the CASE study as a portfolio deliverable. (ActLab p.1)

questions.stem: Reading the Ericsson matrix, a student puts FN = 0 and FP = 64. Why is that swapped?

options.text: Rows = predicted, columns = actual; FN (predicted-unchanged but actually-changed) = 64, and FP = 0

questions.explanation: With rows=predicted, columns=actual: FP=0 and FN=64 (the dangerous misses). Don't swap them; total = 136. (Actualization p.24-25)

questions.stem: Wiring the new Cashiers class into the PoS launch path corresponds structurally to which incorporation shape?

options.text: Incorporating a new supplier (the launch/Store client now points at Cashiers)

questions.explanation: The launch/Store client now points at the new Cashiers supplier, so it is the 'incorporating a new supplier' shape. (Actualization p.11-14)

questions.stem: What is the consequence of violating the Dependency Rule by letting business rules depend on the database?

options.text: The business rules become more testable
The UI becomes independent of the DB
A change to the DB can ripple into the business rules, defeating the architecture's independence
Nothing - the rule is only a style preference

questions.explanation: If business rules depend outward on the DB, a DB change propagates inward into the rules - the exact coupling the inward Dependency Rule prevents. (Clean Architecture p.8, p.16-18)

questions.stem: How does the Composite Reuse Principle contrast inheritance with composition?

options.text: It favors inheritance as the primary reuse mechanism
It treats inheritance and composition as identical
It favors composition/delegation over inheritance, calling inheritance-as-primary-reuse catastrophic

questions.explanation: CRP favors polymorphic composition/delegation over inheritance, calling inheritance-as-primary-reuse a catastrophic mistake. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: In the workflow, when is the precision/recall of Impact Analysis actually scored?

options.text: After change propagation finishes, by comparing predicted vs actual changed classes
Only if the change is small

questions.explanation: After propagation completes you compare predicted vs actual changes and score precision/recall - propagation is the 'moment of truth'. (Actualization p.23)

questions.stem: Which operation, related to incorporation, has a new class take over an old class's role with all references rerouted?
questions.explanation: Replacement of a class has a new class take over an old one's role; references are rerouted and the obsolete class's deletion then propagates. (Actualization p.15)

questions.stem: What is the maintenance consequence of writing business rules as stored procedures in the database?

options.text: Business logic becomes independent of the DB
The View becomes harder to test
Business logic is welded to the DB, so it cannot be reused or tested independently and a DB swap drags the rules along
Precision of impact analysis automatically rises

questions.explanation: Stored-procedure business rules tie logic to the DB, breaking the 'DB is a detail/plug-in' principle and coupling rules to the storage choice. (Clean Architecture p.17)

questions.stem: How do the GRASP Controller and the GUI View classes differ in responsibility for system events?

options.text: Both should directly handle system events
The View handles system events; the Controller only displays data
The Controller handles system events; View/window classes must delegate them to a controller

questions.explanation: Controller receives/handles system events; window/view/widget/document classes are excluded and must delegate. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson matrix, the value 64 sits in which cell, and what does it represent?

options.text: Predicted-unchanged / actually-changed; a false negative (a dangerous miss)

questions.explanation: With rows=predicted, columns=actual, 64 is FN: predicted-unchanged but actually-changed - the dangerous misses. (Actualization p.24-25)

questions.stem: Why does writing new classes separately BEFORE incorporating them help during actualization?

options.text: It lets you get the new classes right and testable in isolation before ripple effects begin at incorporation
It removes the need for impact analysis
It deletes the old code automatically

questions.explanation: Building new code in isolation lets you make it correct and testable; ripple only starts at incorporation. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: What is the consequence of making the database the CENTER of the architecture (rather than a plug-in)?

options.text: The business rules become independent of the DB
The business rules end up depending on a volatile detail, so DB changes ripple into the rules
The View becomes the most stable component

questions.explanation: If the DB is the center, stable business rules depend on a volatile detail, so DB changes ripple inward - the opposite of the intended independence. (Clean Architecture p.16-17)

questions.stem: How do GRASP Low Coupling and High Cohesion differ as quality goals?

options.text: Low Coupling targets a class doing many unrelated things; High Cohesion targets reliance on many classes
Low Coupling concerns interfaces; High Cohesion concerns inheritance
Low Coupling minimises a class's reliance on many other classes; High Cohesion keeps a class's responsibilities focused and related

questions.explanation: Low Coupling reduces reliance on other classes; High Cohesion keeps a class's responsibilities focused (not doing many unrelated things). (OOPrinciples p.21-22)

questions.stem: A perfect-precision prediction flagged 30 classes and all 30 changed, but 64 other classes also changed unflagged. Which describes this exactly?

options.text: High precision, low recall (the Ericsson profile)

questions.explanation: All flagged classes changed (precision 100%) but 64 changed classes were missed (recall 30/94 = 32%) - the Ericsson profile. (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: In the propagation walk's terminology, a class whose supplier changed and which has not yet been updated is described as what?
questions.explanation: A dependent of a modified class that hasn't been updated is inconsistent; propagation visits it until consistency returns. (Actualization p.17-21)

questions.stem: To keep the DB reachable while still pointing dependencies inward, which design element is the best fit?

options.text: A stored procedure holding the business rules
Letting entities call the Database API directly
An Entity Gateway interface declared with the business rules and implemented in the detail layer
Putting the DB in the Entities layer

questions.explanation: The Entity Gateway (Repository) interface lives with the rules; its implementation lives in the detail layer, so the dependency still points inward (DIP). (Clean Architecture p.18)

questions.stem: How does the Presenter differ from the View in the Clean Architecture data flow?

options.text: The Presenter just displays data; the View translates the response model
The Presenter translates the Response Model into a View Model (strings/flags); the View only displays the View Model

questions.explanation: The Presenter (MVP) translates the Response Model into a View Model of display-ready strings/flags; the View just shows it. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: A manager asks which Ericsson metric to watch for schedule risk. Why is recall the answer, not precision?

options.text: Recall captures missed (false-negative) classes; missing them means unplanned work that blows schedules, whereas high precision is comparatively easy
Precision counts missed classes, which blow schedules
Recall measures false alarms, which waste time
Both are equally relevant to schedule risk

questions.explanation: Recall reflects missed changed classes (false negatives) = unplanned effort = schedule risk; precision (no false alarms) is easy. (Actualization p.27-28)

questions.stem: Why does the deck call change propagation the 'moment of truth' rather than impact analysis itself?

options.text: Because propagation predicts the impact set
Because propagation actually modifies the impacted code, confirming or refuting Impact Analysis's predictions
Because propagation happens before impact analysis
Because propagation has no relationship to impact analysis

questions.explanation: Propagation modifies the impacted classes, confirming or refuting the earlier predictions - the moment of truth. (Actualization p.23)

questions.stem: A student treats 'architecture' and 'design pattern' as synonyms. Why does the deck reject this?

options.text: The deck says they are identical
MVC/MVVM/MVP/Singleton/Observer are patterns; Hexagonal/Onion/Clean are architectures - patterns solve local problems, architecture organises the whole system

questions.explanation: The deck separates them: patterns (Singleton/Observer/MVC...) solve local design problems; architectures (Hexagonal/Onion/Clean) organise the whole system. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: Ostrich extends Bird, but its fly() throws UnsupportedOperationException, breaking any client that calls fly(). Which principle is broken?
questions.explanation: A subtype that cannot honour the base contract (fly) is not substitutable for its base - LSP violation. (OOPrinciples p.8-9)

questions.stem: If a prediction achieves recall = 100% but precision = 60%, what does that imply about the prediction's errors?

options.text: It caught every changed class but also flagged some classes that did not change (false positives)
It made no errors at all
It is identical to the Ericsson result

questions.explanation: Recall 100% = no misses; precision 60% = some false alarms (false positives among predictions). (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: A developer thinks 'outer layers are stable, so dependencies should point outward.' Why is this backwards?

options.text: It is correct; dependencies point outward
Inner layers are the stable abstractions, so dependencies point INWARD; outer layers are the volatile details

questions.explanation: Inner = stable abstractions (Entities); outer = volatile details. Dependencies point inward so details depend on abstractions. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: To support a new shape, a developer must edit an existing switch statement inside a render() method rather than add a class. Which principle does this break?
questions.explanation: Having to modify existing code to add a feature violates OCP - software entities should be open for extension, closed for modification. (OOPrinciples p.6)

questions.stem: A developer wants to write thorough unit tests for the View. Why does the deck push back?

options.text: The View holds the most complex logic and must be tested first
Views cannot be tested by definition
Testing the View would violate SRP
The View should be 'so stupid you don't have to test it' - all formatting is in the Presenter, leaving the View trivial

questions.explanation: Because the Presenter does all formatting, the View just displays view-model data and is meant to be so trivial you don't test it. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: Code does customer.getAccount().getBalance().getAmount() - a 'train wreck' chained through objects returned by method calls. Which principle is violated?

options.text: Principle of Least Knowledge / Law of Demeter

questions.explanation: Chaining through transitively-obtained objects violates the Law of Demeter; talk only to immediate collaborators. (OOPrinciples p.15)

questions.stem: What is the correct INBOUND data-flow order before the interactor runs?
questions.explanation: Inbound: the Controller packs input, passes it through the input Boundary to the Interactor, which controls the Entities. (Clean Architecture p.10-11, p.14)

questions.stem: A Square extends Rectangle and overrides setWidth() to also set the height, breaking callers that adjust width and height separately. Which principle does this break?
questions.explanation: The Square subtype breaks the Rectangle contract clients rely on - it is not substitutable, violating LSP. (OOPrinciples p.8)

questions.stem: What is the correct OUTBOUND data-flow order after the interactor produces results?

options.text: View Model -> Response Model -> Presenter -> View
Response Model -> output Boundary -> Presenter -> View Model -> View

questions.explanation: Outbound: Response Model passes out via the output Boundary to the Presenter, which builds the View Model that the View displays. (Clean Architecture p.13-15)

questions.stem: Given the responsibility 'create an instance of A by the class that aggregates A', which GRASP pattern is being described?
questions.explanation: Assigning creation of A to a class that aggregates/contains A is the Creator pattern. (OOPrinciples p.20)

questions.stem: A developer asks where Presenters, ViewModels and Gateways/Repositories live. Which layer is correct?
questions.explanation: Presenters (MVP), ViewModels (MVVM) and Gateways/Repositories all live in the Interface Adapters layer. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: Given the responsibility 'mediate between two components via an intermediate object so they are not directly coupled', which GRASP pattern is it?
questions.explanation: Using an intermediate object to decouple components is the Indirection pattern (beware transitive visibility). (OOPrinciples p.25)

questions.stem: Why does the interactor receive the Request Model when it crosses the input boundary?

options.text: Because the View calls the interactor directly
Because the entities own the boundary
Because the interactor IMPLEMENTS the input boundary interface, so data passing through it arrives at the interactor

questions.explanation: The interactor implements the input boundary, so the Request Model passing through that boundary is received by the interactor. (Clean Architecture p.9-10)

questions.stem: Given the responsibility 'choose behaviour at runtime based on the receiver's type via polymorphic operations', which GRASP pattern is it?
questions.explanation: Assigning type-varying behaviour to polymorphic operations on a base class/interface is GRASP Polymorphism. (OOPrinciples p.24)

questions.stem: Which memory hook correctly orders the layers outer-to-inner with the dependency direction noted?

options.text: Entities -> Use Cases -> Adapters -> Frameworks, dependencies outward
Frameworks -> Interface Adapters -> Use Cases -> Entities, dependencies flow inward
Frameworks -> Entities -> Adapters -> Use Cases, dependencies outward
Use Cases -> Frameworks -> Entities -> Adapters, dependencies inward

questions.explanation: Outer to inner: Frameworks, Interface Adapters, Use Cases, Entities; dependencies flow inward. (Clean Architecture p.5-8)

questions.stem: Given the responsibility 'wrap a point of predicted instability behind a stable access interface', which GRASP pattern is it?
questions.explanation: Creating a stable interface around a predicted point of variation is Protected Variations. (OOPrinciples p.27)

questions.stem: A request says 'add a completely new web framework as the delivery mechanism.' In a clean design, the business rules should require what change?

options.text: A full rewrite of the entities
No change to the business rules - the framework is an outer-layer detail that plugs in

questions.explanation: Frameworks are outer-layer details; business rules stay independent of frameworks, so they need no change. (Clean Architecture p.4, p.7)

questions.stem: What is the consequence of a high-coupling design, per GRASP Low Coupling?

options.text: Changes in related classes force local changes, and classes are harder to understand in isolation and harder to reuse
Changes are perfectly localised and classes are easy to reuse

questions.explanation: High coupling means changes elsewhere force local changes, and classes are hard to understand and reuse in isolation. (OOPrinciples p.21)

questions.stem: Why does the deck say the View should hold only strings and flags from the View Model rather than domain objects?

options.text: Because domain objects are faster to render
So the View stays trivial ('so stupid you don't test it') with all formatting decided earlier by the Presenter
Because the View owns the business rules
Because flags violate the Dependency Rule

questions.explanation: The View Model holds display-ready strings/flags so the View is trivial and needs no testing; the Presenter did the formatting. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: What is the consequence of using a single fat interface (violating ISP) when one method's signature changes?

options.text: Only the one client using that method is affected
The change ripples to ALL implementers of the fat interface, even those not using that method

questions.explanation: A fat interface couples all implementers; a signature change ripples to every implementer. Segregated interfaces confine the ripple. (OOPrinciples p.10-11)

questions.stem: A new requirement: 'support a NoSQL store in addition to SQL.' Which GRASP pattern AND which Clean Architecture element together make this a localized change?

options.text: Information Expert and the Request Model
Protected Variations and the Entity Gateway/Repository interface

questions.explanation: Protected Variations wraps the store choice; the Entity Gateway/Repository localizes the swap to its implementation. (OOPrinciples p.27; Clean Architecture p.18)

questions.stem: What is the maintenance consequence of using inheritance as the primary reuse mechanism, per CRP?

options.text: It is a catastrophic mistake that spreads change up and down the inheritance tree
Change is perfectly localised by swapping a delegate
It guarantees the Law of Demeter is satisfied

questions.explanation: CRP calls inheritance-as-primary-reuse catastrophic; deep hierarchies spread change up and down the tree, unlike composition which localises it. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: What is the consequence of a low-cohesion class (doing many unrelated things), per GRASP High Cohesion?

options.text: It is easy to comprehend, reuse, and maintain
It is hard to comprehend, hard to reuse, hard to maintain, and delicate (constantly affected by change)

questions.explanation: Low cohesion makes a class hard to comprehend/reuse/maintain and delicate - constantly affected by change. (OOPrinciples p.22)

questions.stem: Behaviour varies by the type of figure (rectangle vs ellipse vs line). Which GRASP pattern best assigns the draw() responsibility?

options.text: Polymorphism - define the operation on a base class/interface and let subtypes vary it

questions.explanation: When behaviour varies by type, GRASP Polymorphism assigns it via polymorphic operations on a common base/interface. (OOPrinciples p.24)

questions.stem: You need persistence logic but don't want it inside any domain entity. Which GRASP pattern best assigns it?

options.text: Pure Fabrication - a made-up cohesive class like PersistentStorage

questions.explanation: Pure Fabrication invents a non-domain cohesive class (e.g. PersistentStorage) to hold persistence, keeping entities clean. (OOPrinciples p.26)

questions.stem: Two components should not be directly coupled. Which GRASP pattern best assigns the mediating responsibility?

options.text: Indirection - assign mediation to an intermediate object

questions.explanation: Indirection introduces an intermediate object to decouple two components (mediator/adapter style). (OOPrinciples p.25)

questions.stem: A new TaxCalculator class becomes a supplier that several existing client classes must now point to. The maintainer worries this is where ripple will be worst. Why is that intuition correct?

options.text: Because suppliers can never be tested in isolation
Because every existing client of the new supplier may need adjustment
Because adding a supplier always requires deleting an old class
Because suppliers sit in the outermost architecture layer

questions.explanation: In the 'incorporating a new supplier' shape, every client that now points to the supplier may need adjustment, so propagation is most likely there. (Actualization p.11)

questions.stem: Pure Fabrication and Information Expert can conflict. How does the deck resolve who saves an entity to the DB?

options.text: Information Expert wins: the entity saves itself
Both are ignored; the Controller saves
Pure Fabrication wins: a made-up class like PersistentStorage handles saving to keep cohesion high
Low Coupling forbids any save logic

questions.explanation: Although the entity 'has the data' (Expert), assigning persistence to a made-up PersistentStorage (Pure Fabrication) keeps cohesion high and coupling low. (OOPrinciples p.26)

questions.stem: A new impact prediction yields TP=18, FP=0, FN=2. Compared with Ericsson, which interpretation is correct?

options.text: Precision is 100% (like Ericsson) and recall is 90% (far better than Ericsson's 32%)

questions.explanation: Precision = 18/18 = 100%; recall = 18/(18+2) = 90% - same perfect precision as Ericsson but much higher recall. (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: Why is the inward Dependency Rule equivalent to applying DIP at architecture scale?

options.text: Because both make concrete details depend on stable abstractions, never the reverse
Because both forbid testing the business rules
Because both put the database at the center
Because both eliminate the Use Cases layer

questions.explanation: Pointing dependencies inward means details (outer) depend on abstractions (inner) - exactly DIP, scaled to architecture. (Clean Architecture p.8; OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: A PoS team debates SQL vs NoSQL for credentials and how the sign-in page should look. Why can a clean architecture treat both of these as swappable 'details' rather than core decisions?

options.text: Because the entities directly call the database
Because the login use case is insulated from UI and DB, so those choices plug in behind boundaries and propagation is bounded
Because details are always edited in place as small changes
Because the View must be tested thoroughly

questions.explanation: Insulating the interactor from UI and DB makes the store choice and screen look plug-in details, bounding propagation to the gateway impl and view. (Clean Architecture p.4, p.16-18)

questions.stem: What is the consequence of violating the Law of Demeter (heavy transitive visibility) for change propagation?

options.text: Propagation shrinks because more objects are reachable
Transitive coupling grows, which is exactly the invisible coupling that makes propagation explode

questions.explanation: Transitive visibility creates invisible dependencies - the same kind behind Ericsson's under-estimation - so propagation explodes. (OOPrinciples p.15; Actualization p.28)

questions.stem: If a prediction has TP=10, FP=10, FN=0, what are its precision and recall?
questions.explanation: Precision = 10/(10+10) = 50%; recall = 10/(10+0) = 100% - the inverse of the Ericsson profile. (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: Martin's rule of thumb pairs 'changes a lot' and 'doesn't change often' with which roles?

options.text: Changes a lot -> plug-in; doesn't change often -> plugged into
Changes a lot -> plugged into; doesn't change often -> plug-in

questions.explanation: Martin: if something changes a lot it should be a plug-in; if it doesn't change often it should be plugged into. (Clean Architecture p.16)

questions.stem: A propagation walk over sale/register/saleLineItem/store/item/taxCategory keeps producing new inconsistencies and won't converge. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: The change is too small to propagate
A design problem (e.g. excessive coupling) - termination is the goal and non-convergence signals it
Impact analysis recall is too high

questions.explanation: Propagation should terminate; one that won't converge signals a design problem such as excessive coupling. (Actualization p.21)

questions.stem: A developer wants OCP but keeps editing a concrete class. Which chain of reasoning best explains how to fix it?

options.text: Apply ISP to split the interface, which automatically yields OCP
Apply LSP to make subtypes substitutable, which is the same as OCP
Apply DIP - depend on an abstraction - because the deck says DIP tells us how to adhere to OCP
Apply CRP to replace the class with composition, which is OCP

questions.explanation: The deck states DIP is the mechanism for OCP: depend on abstractions so new implementations extend behaviour without edits. (OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: Two teams predict the same change. Team A: TP=20, FP=0, FN=30. Team B: TP=20, FP=30, FN=0. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Team A has better recall than Team B
Both teams have identical precision and recall
Team B missed more changed classes than Team A
Team A matches the Ericsson profile (perfect precision, low recall); Team B has perfect recall but poor precision

questions.explanation: A: precision=100%, recall=40%; B: precision=40%, recall=100%. A mirrors Ericsson's no-false-alarms/many-misses profile. (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: Although the Entity Gateway eventually reaches the database, what keeps its dependency arrow pointing inward?

options.text: Because the gateway INTERFACE is declared with the business rules, while the IMPLEMENTATION lives outside - so the rules depend only on the abstraction
Because the gateway implementation is in the Entities layer
Because the database calls the entities directly
Because the interface and implementation are both in the outer layer

questions.explanation: The interface sits with the rules; the impl sits in the detail layer, so the rules depend on the abstraction - dependency still inward (DIP). (Clean Architecture p.18)

questions.stem: After a JHotDraw 'add a tool' change, you scored precision=100% but recall=60%. What does this most directly tell the project manager?

options.text: The prediction had many false alarms
Every predicted class really changed, but 40% of the changed classes were missed - effort was under-estimated
Recall and precision were both perfect

questions.explanation: Precision 100% = no false alarms; recall 60% = 40% of changed classes missed, signalling under-estimated effort. (Actualization p.23, p.26-28)

questions.stem: Why can OCP, DIP, and Indirection all be seen as special cases of Protected Variations?

options.text: Because they are all GRASP patterns
Because each wraps a predicted point of change behind a stable seam (abstraction/intermediary)

questions.explanation: Protected Variations is the grand-unifying idea: wrap variation behind a stable interface; OCP, DIP, and Indirection are instances. (OOPrinciples p.27)

questions.stem: Why does the deck argue that high precision is 'easy' but high recall is 'hard'?

options.text: Because precision counts every class twice
Because you can predict few classes confidently (high precision) but invisible dependencies cause you to miss many (low recall)

questions.explanation: Flagging only sure classes gives high precision; invisible dependencies cause missed classes (false negatives), making recall hard. (Actualization p.27-28)

questions.stem: Why must the Request Model and Response Model carry only primitive/plain data rather than UI strings?
