options.text: Because they cross boundaries between layers; keeping them plain keeps the inner layers ignorant of presentation details (the Presenter formats later)
Because primitives are faster to allocate

questions.explanation: These models cross layer boundaries; plain data keeps inner layers presentation-agnostic - the Presenter turns the Response Model into display strings. (Clean Architecture p.10, p.15)

questions.stem: How does the Pure Fabrication PersistentStorage example correspond to Clean Architecture?

options.text: It is the same idea as the Gateway/Repository that keeps persistence out of domain entities

questions.explanation: PersistentStorage (Pure Fabrication) keeps persistence out of entities - the same role as the Clean Architecture Gateway/Repository. (OOPrinciples p.26; Clean Architecture p.18)

questions.stem: A student claims under-estimation of impact is a flaw unique to inexperienced programmers. Why is this wrong, per the deck?

options.text: It only happens for small changes
Under-estimation is systemic - a consequence of invisibility (you can't see all dependencies) - and is common in other fields too
It is caused by too-high recall

questions.explanation: The deck says under-estimation is systemic, a consequence of invisibility, and common in other fields - not an experience issue. (Actualization p.28)

questions.stem: A team puts validation business rules into both the UI and stored procedures 'for convenience.' Which two deck principles does this violate at once?

options.text: Independent of frameworks and Testable only
Independent of UI and Independent of database (business rules belong in the inner layers, not UI or stored procedures)

questions.explanation: Rules in the UI break UI-independence; rules in stored procedures break DB-independence - both push business rules into outer details. (Clean Architecture p.4, p.17)

questions.stem: A maintainer reduces transitive 'train wreck' chains. Which combined benefit does the deck predict for maintenance?

options.text: Lower transitive coupling, so the invisible dependencies behind impact under-estimation shrink and propagation is shorter
No change to propagation at all

questions.explanation: Following Demeter cuts transitive coupling - the invisible dependencies behind under-estimation - shortening propagation. (OOPrinciples p.15; Actualization p.28)

questions.stem: Which single statement best captures why SOLID + GRASP together make actualization cheaper?

options.text: They increase inheritance depth so changes propagate faster
They lower coupling and put variation behind stable interfaces, so incorporating new code ripples less and propagation terminates sooner
They eliminate the need for impact analysis
They make every class depend on the database

questions.explanation: Lower coupling plus variation behind stable seams (OCP/DIP/Protected Variations) shortens ripple and improves recall. (OOPrinciples p.3-27 synthesis)

questions.stem: GRASP Polymorphism, OCP, and the actualization 'add a Pig' technique are unified by which single idea?

options.text: Extend behaviour by adding a type/subclass rather than modifying existing conditional code
Put business rules in the database

questions.explanation: All three say: add a new type to extend behaviour instead of editing a type-switching conditional. (Actualization p.6-7; OOPrinciples p.6, p.24)

questions.stem: Which two GRASP patterns are evaluative quality goals rather than concrete structural recipes?
questions.explanation: Low Coupling and High Cohesion are evaluative goals used to judge a design, not structural recipes. (OOPrinciples p.21-22)

questions.stem: A reviewer says 'this Square/Rectangle bug, the Ostrich/fly bug, and a subtype that tightens a precondition are all the same category of defect.' Which principle unifies them?

options.text: Liskov Substitution Principle - all break the base contract clients rely on

questions.explanation: All three are substitutability failures: the subtype breaks the base contract, the hallmark LSP violation. (OOPrinciples p.8-9)

questions.stem: Why does the deck call inheritance-as-primary-reuse 'catastrophic' yet still use inheritance in the Farm/Pig and OCP examples?

options.text: Inheritance is wrong as the PRIMARY reuse mechanism, but correct for genuine is-a polymorphism, which is what Farm/Pig and OCP use
The Farm/Pig example actually uses composition

questions.explanation: CRP condemns inheritance-as-reuse, not is-a polymorphism; Farm/Pig and OCP use legitimate is-a subtyping. (OOPrinciples p.14; Actualization p.6-7)
